1: Manasseh was twelve years old at his accession and reigned fifty-five years — the longest reign in the Judean monarchy. The extreme length is theologically loaded: the Deuteronomistic History frames it as evidence that YHWH’s patience is not indifference. Ḥizqiyyāhû (Hezekiah) and Mᵉnaššeh (Manasseh) are placed in immediate succession: the son of the most faithful Judean king becomes the most faithless. The name Mᵉnaššeh means “one who causes to forget” — a name the narrative validates, as he forgets everything his father restored.
2: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, following the tôʿăḇōt (detestable practices) of the nations YHWH had driven out.” The tôʿăḇôt (singular tôʿēḇāh) is the standard Deuteronomic/Levitical term for cultic abomination — actions structurally incompatible with covenant holiness (Deut 12:31; 18:9-12; Lev 18:22-30). The comparison is inverted from the usual formula: normally Manasseh is judged against David; here he is measured against the Canaanites whom YHWH expelled. “More evil than the nations YHWH had destroyed before Israel” (v9) will sharpen this reversal. The one who held covenant land must not replicate the practices that made the previous occupants forfeit it.
3: Manasseh “rebuilt the high places his father Hezekiah had demolished.” The verb bānāh (built/rebuilt) echoes ironically against Hezekiah’s hēsîr (removed) in 18:4. What Hezekiah tore down, Manasseh rebuilds; what Hezekiah threw out, Manasseh sets up. The pairing with “as Ahab king of Israel had done” is the Deuteronomistic History’s harshest condemnation of a Judean king: he is measured not against the bad kings of Judah but against the worst of the northern dynasty. The ʾăšērāh pole returns — the cult object of the fertility goddess Asherah, the consort of El/Baal in Canaanite religion, whose presence in Judah was a direct violation of the first two commandments.
4: “He built altars in the house of YHWH, of which YHWH had said, ‘In Jerusalem I will put my Name.’” The phrase “put my Name” (lāśûm šᵉmî) is the Deuteronomic formula for YHWH’s unique dwelling choice (Deut 12:5, 11, 21; 14:23-24; 1 Kgs 8:29). To erect pagan altars in the Name’s specific dwelling is not merely syncretism but a direct assault on the covenantal address-point of YHWH’s presence. The temple altars to Baal occupy the space consecrated to the Name.
5: “In both courts of the house of YHWH he built altars to all the starry hosts.” Ṣeḇāʾ haššāmayim — “host of heaven/starry hosts” — refers to the astral cult of Mesopotamian origin: worship of sun, moon, planets, and stars as deities or divine intermediaries. Deut 4:19 explicitly warns against being “drawn away to bow down to them and worship them.” Mesopotamian astral religion had penetrated the Levant through Assyrian cultural pressure during the 7th century; Manasseh’s reign (697-642 BCE) coincided with the height of Neo-Assyrian dominance, and his astral cult may reflect political as well as religious conformity to the overlord.
6: The catalogue of Manasseh’s abominations reaches its peak: “He sacrificed his son in the fire (heʿĕḇîr ʾet bᵉnô bāʾēš), practiced divination (ʿônēn), sought omens (niḥēš), and consulted mediums (ʾōḇôt) and spiritists (yiddᵉʿōnîm).” The verb heʿĕḇîr bāʾēš — “passed through fire” — is the standard idiom for child sacrifice to Molech (Lev 18:21; 20:2-5; Deut 18:10). ʿōnēn (cloud-reading/soothsaying), nāḥaš (divination by omens/serpents), ʾōḇ (medium/ghost-consulter), and yiddᵉʿōnî (spiritist/one with a familiar spirit) — this list precisely matches the prohibitions of Deut 18:10-11. Manasseh does not miss a single item on the Deuteronomic list of forbidden practices.
7: The carved Asherah pole (pesel hāʾăšērāh) is placed inside the temple itself — the holy of holies’ antechamber. The specific violation: Deut 16:21 explicitly prohibits planting an Asherah “beside the altar of YHWH your God.” The divine word quoted — “In this house and in Jerusalem, which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel, I will put my Name forever” — is the election formula at its most emphatic: “forever” (lᵉʿôlām). The permanence of YHWH’s election is the standard against which Manasseh’s desecration is measured. The idol stands in the place of the Name.
8: The conditional promise: “I will no longer make Israel’s feet wander from the land I gave their ancestors — only if they carefully observe everything I commanded them, all the law my servant Moses commanded.” The raq (“only if”) introduces the condition; šmr in the infinitive absolute construction (šāmᵉrû lāʿăśôt) — “diligently observing to do” — expresses the intensity required. This conditional promise is quoted at the moment of Manasseh’s maximum violation to make the contrast explicit: the promise existed; the condition was not met; the consequence will follow.
9: “But they did not listen, and Manasseh led them astray (wayyatʿēm) so that they did more evil than the nations YHWH had destroyed before Israel.” Tāʿāh (causative: to cause to err/wander) is the same root used of the false prophet who “leads you astray” in Deut 13:5. Manasseh functions as a false prophet in royal office — he is the king as the anti-Moses. The comparison to “the nations YHWH had destroyed” is the strongest possible indictment: Israel has descended below the covenant-free pagans who preceded them. Greater privilege = greater accountability (cf. Amos 3:2).
10: “The LORD said through his servants the prophets” — the plural marks that this is not an isolated oracle but the sustained prophetic witness of the period (Isaiah, Micah, and the unnamed prophets of the Assyrian era all preached in this context). The indirect delivery — the narrator summarizes rather than quoting individual oracles — is the Deuteronomistic History’s way of showing that the prophetic word was pervasive and unavoidable. The disaster that follows is not unforeseen; it was announced.
11: The prophetic indictment names three grounds: (1) ḥiqqûṣîm — “detestable acts” (from šāqaṣ, to abhor/detest — a stronger term than tôʿēḇāh); (2) “more evil than the Amorites before him” — the comparison restated; (3) “made Judah sin with his idols (gillûlîm).” Gillûlîm — a deliberately contemptuous term for idols, likely derived from gēl (dung-pellets) — is Ezekiel’s characteristic pejorative for pagan cult objects (used 39 times in Ezekiel). Its use here links Manasseh’s idols to the language of the exile prophets who interpreted the destruction Manasseh brought on.
12: “I am going to bring such disaster on Jerusalem and Judah that the ears of everyone who hears about it will tingle.” Wəṣilᵉlāh šᵉtê ʾoznāyw — “both his ears will tingle/ring.” The formula occurs in 1 Sam 3:11 (Eli’s house) and Jer 19:3 (Jerusalem’s destruction) — it marks an event so catastrophic that merely hearing about it produces a physical sensation of shock. The oracle announces what will become the Babylonian exile without yet naming the agent.
13: “I will stretch over Jerusalem the measuring line used against Samaria and the plumb line used against the house of Ahab.” Qaw šōmᵉrôn (Samaria’s measuring line) and mišqelet bêt ʾaḥʾāḇ (Ahab’s plumb line) are the architect’s instruments — used here as demolition tools. When a builder stretches a line and drops a plumb, he is measuring whether the wall is true; here YHWH is measuring Jerusalem against the same standard he applied to Samaria (destroyed 722 BCE) and Ahab’s house (destroyed within a generation). The image continues: “I will wipe Jerusalem as one wipes a dish, wiping it and turning it upside down” — the ordinary household act of cleaning becomes an image of total erasure.
14: “I will abandon the remnant of my inheritance (šᵉʾērît naḥălātî) and hand them over to their enemies.” Naḥălāh (inheritance/portion) is the covenantal term for Israel as YHWH’s share among the nations (Deut 4:20; 9:26, 29; 32:9). YHWH’s “abandoning” (nāṭaš) his inheritance is the sharpest possible reversal of the covenant: the God who “does not forsake his people” (1 Sam 12:22; Ps 94:14) announces that covenant forsaking has been so complete on Israel’s side that the relationship is structurally terminated. The šᵉʾērît (remnant) had been the language of hope; here it is the object of abandonment.
15: The reason for the abandonment is rooted in the Exodus itself: “from the day their ancestors came out of Egypt until this day.” The rebellion is not a recent development but a lifelong pattern — the Deuteronomistic History’s most comprehensive statement of Israel’s chronic covenant failure. The phrase “provoking my anger” (makhʿîsîm ʾōtî) — causing YHWH grief/anger — is the emotional register of divine relationship: the abandoned covenant partner.
16: “Moreover, Manasseh shed so much innocent blood (dām nāqî) that he filled Jerusalem from end to end.” Dām nāqî — “innocent blood” — is the specific term for judicial murder and extrajudicial killing (Gen 9:6; Deut 19:10; Jer 7:6; 22:3). The Targum and rabbinic tradition identify Manasseh’s killing of prophets, specifically Isaiah, as among these acts. The “from end to end” (peh ʾel peh — literally “mouth to mouth”) is a measure of completeness: the innocent blood saturated the city. The shedding of innocent blood is identified in the Deuteronomistic History as the sin that ultimately could not be atoned (24:4).
17: The regnal summary formula: acts and sins recorded in the annals. The notation “including the sin he committed” (wəḥaṭṭāʾtô ʾăšer ḥāṭāʾ) — “his sin which he sinned” — is an unusually redundant phrasing that the narrator uses to underscore that the annals contain incriminating material, not merely administrative record.
18: Manasseh is buried in the garden of his palace — gān ʿuzzāʾ, the Garden of Uzza — not in the City of David where the covenant kings were buried. The location of burial outside the royal tombs is theologically significant in Kings: the kings buried in the city of David are implicitly within the covenant succession; those buried elsewhere are distanced from it. Amon will be buried in the same garden (v26).
19: Amon: twenty-two years old, reigned two years. The brevity of his reign — two years — stands in stark contrast to his father’s fifty-five. The short reign is both historical fact and literary comment: Manasseh’s dynasty could not sustain itself. The mother’s name, Meshullemeth daughter of Haruz of Jotbah, is preserved — the Deuteronomistic History maintains the maternal genealogy for all Judean kings.
20: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, as his father Manasseh had done.” The evaluative formula for Amon is the simplest possible: he repeated his father’s pattern without variation. The interesting contrast with Manasseh is that Amon receives no extended prophetic oracle and no attention to specific sins — his reign is too brief to establish independent theological significance. He is the continuation of Manasseh’s trajectory without Manasseh’s length.
21: “He walked in all the way his father walked, worshipping the idols (gillûlîm) his father had worshipped and bowing down to them.” Hālak bəderek — “to walk in the way of” — is the Deuteronomistic History’s standard moral evaluation formula. The negative form (“did not walk in the way of David”) is the condemnation; the positive form (“walked in all the way of his father”) — when the father is Manasseh — is equally damning. The gillûlîm recurs from v11: Amon inherits both the idols and the contempt they deserve.
22: “He abandoned (ʿāzaḇ) YHWH, the God of his ancestors, and did not walk in the way of YHWH.” ʿāzaḇ — “to abandon/forsake” — is the covenantal term for covenant desertion (Deut 28:20; 31:16; Jer 1:16). The contrast with Josiah’s mother’s name in 22:1 (Yᵉḏîḏāh — “beloved of YHWH”) is the narrator’s subtle marker of what is coming: out of Amon’s house comes the beloved king.
23: Amon’s officials (ʿăḇādāyw — his servants/courtiers) conspire and assassinate him in his palace. The conspiracy is palace-internal — not a popular revolt but a court coup. The motive is not stated; later scholarship has proposed anti-Assyrian or pro-reform factions, but the text does not explain. The narrator is interested in the theological, not the political, mechanism.
24: “The people of the land (ʿam hāʾāreṣ) killed all who had conspired against King Amon and made his son Josiah king in his place.” The ʿam hāʾāreṣ — “people of the land” — is a recurring political actor in Kings: the free landowning citizenry who function as a constitutional check on palace coups (cf. 11:14, 18-20; 14:21; 16:15). They punish the assassins and install the legitimate heir. Their action here protects the Davidic succession that YHWH promised to preserve and ensures that Josiah, not a usurper, occupies the throne.
25: Standard negative regnal summary for Amon. The brevity of the notice matches the brevity of the reign.
26: Amon buried in the Garden of Uzza — the same location as Manasseh (v18). Father and son share the garden burial, both excluded from the City of David’s covenant tombs. The dynasty resumes with Josiah, the great-grandson of Manasseh, who will reverse everything his ancestor set up.
